Unit 5: The Accusative Case & Basic Prepositions

In Unit 2, you learned that when a noun is the Object (receiving the action), it drops its final -ς (if masculine) and its article becomes τον, την, or το.

IMPORTANT

The Masculine 'N'
We always keep the final -ν in the masculine article (τον) to avoid confusion with the neuter article (το). This is true even if the next word starts with a soft sound!

Now, we will see the Accusative Case in action through verbs and the "Big Three" prepositions.

1. Verbs that use the Accusative

Most "action" verbs require the object to be in the Accusative case. - Βλέπω τον Γιώργο. (I see George). - Έχω την τσάντα. (I have the bag). - Διαβάζω το βιβλίο. (I read the book).

CAUTION

Remember: The verb Είμαι (I am) is special. It acts as a mirror, so it keeps everything in the Nominative (ο, η, το).
- Είμαι ο Γιώργος. (I am George).

2. The Big Three Prepositions (σε, από, με)

In Greek, almost all prepositions force the noun that follows them into the Accusative case.

A. Σε (To / At / In)

This word shows location or direction. In Greek, we almost never say "σε" and "the" separately. They merge into one word: - σε + τον = στον - σε + την = στη(ν) - σε + το = στο

Example: Πάω στο σινεμά. (I am going to the cinema).

B. Από (From)

Used for origins. It does not merge with the article, but the article must still be in the Accusative. - Είμαι από την Ελλάδα. (I am from Greece). - Έρχομαι από τον γιατρό. (I am coming from the doctor).

TIP

Blend it! Even though it's written separately, Greeks often blend them when speaking quickly (από το sounds like "ap-to")!

C. Με (With)

Used for company or instruments. - Είμαι με τη Μαρία. (I am with Maria). - Θέλω καφέ με ζάχαρη. (I want coffee with sugar).

3. Pronunciation: The Dropping 'N'

While the masculine articles (τον, στον, έναν) always keep their -ν in writing to avoid confusion with the neuter, the feminine words (την, στην) and particles (δεν, μην) are more selective. They only keep the -ν if the next word starts with: 1. A vowel (α, ε, η, ι, ο, υ, ω) 2. A hard consonant (κ, π, τ, ξ, ψ) 3. A digraph (μπ, ντ, γκ, τσ, τζ)

  • Στη δασκάλα (The teacher - soft 'δ')
  • Στην πόρτα (The door - hard 'π')

If you aren't sure, it is usually safer to include the -ν—native speakers will still understand you perfectly!

🎯 Unit 5 Mastery Checklist

  • I can use βλέπω, έχω, and θέλω with the correct Accusative articles.
  • I know that σε + την = στην.
  • I remember to drop the -ς for masculine nouns after από and με.

Practice Vocabulary: A1 Vocabulary: Survival Phrases
